(7) In his local dialect, we are told, theBhrftgara, Vase, was known
as Darhddni or Damani (LXXX.6), We are not in a position
to affirm whether the word was in vogue in Kashmir or else-
where. We have been told, however, that that word is used in
Eastern Hindi for Guldastd (vase).
(8) He explains the North Indian custom of building earthen
granaries in the following words FTT^."
Even now we can sec in the villages such mud granaries stand-
ing near fields. In Eastern Hindi it is called, they say, Dehari
or Bakhdr.
(9) He interprets the word Svapacah (in LIII.84) as Domah. Even
now outcastes are called by this term in Eastern Hindi.
(10) The word Sahdydn (in GIV.35) meaning companions or help-
mates, is construed by him as Sdrthinah, We cannot make out
